preamble: The loss of a nasal consonant is an extremely common sound change, often leaving its mark through the nasalization of the preceding vowel or the compensatory lengthening of the vowel.

examples.note: Baltic lost the nasal *n before word-final *-s; e.g., PIE *-ons → Lithuanian '-us' (masc. acc. pl.), with compensatory lengthening of the vowel.
Latin final m/n was lost, leaving the preceding vowel nasalized
Proto-Germanic nasal lost before fricatives (Ingvaeonic Nasal Spirant Law)
Nasal lost with compensatory lengthening of the vowel

Khalkha Mongolian exhibits variable loss of stem-final *-n in citation forms. Proto-Mongolic stems ending in *-n often surface without the nasal in the nominative, though it reappears before vowel-initial suffixes (e.g., possessive, accusative). This represents a morphophonological alternation between zero (Ø) in absolute final position and /n/ before suffixes.
Comparative evidence from Khorchin Mongolian, which preserves more stem-final nasals, supports the reconstruction of *-n that was subsequently deleted in Khalkha.

sources: Germanic Historical Linguistics: Ingvaeonic Nasal Spirant Law
